<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] The breadth of CLI command coverage (ci, config, dev, plan, start, status, sync, usage, work) means this zone is the primary regression barrier for the orchestration tier — keeping tests fast and deterministic is essential.
[observation] [info] Zero coupling across 21 test files confirms the E2E suite treats the CLI as a true black box, which maximises its value as a refactoring safety net.
[observation] [info] architecture-policy.test.js enforces the four-tier import hierarchy at runtime; any new cross-package import path should be validated here first before merging.
[pattern] [info] architecture-policy.test.js is the only zone in the entire codebase that enforces the four-tier dependency hierarchy as a runtime assertion — it is a single point of failure for import-boundary enforcement; consider whether additional policy checks (e.g. gateway module purity) should live here
[anti-pattern] [critical] architecture-policy.test.js is a single point of failure for four-tier hierarchy enforcement. If this file is skipped, broken, or omitted from a CI run, cross-tier import violations can merge undetected. The policy should be backed by at least one redundant mechanism (e.g. an eslint import boundary rule or a dedicated CI step that runs before package tests).
[suggestion] [warning] Add an e2e test for the complete MCP HTTP workflow: start server, verify /mcp/rex and /mcp/sourcevision respond to tool calls, stop server. This is the recommended transport per CLAUDE.md but has no e2e coverage, meaning regressions in the HTTP MCP layer are invisible to the test suite.
[suggestion] [info] architecture-policy.test.js enforces cross-tier import boundaries but not the spawn-only rule for orchestration-tier scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js). A companion check that scans orchestration-tier files for require/import statements targeting package internals would close the gap between import-boundary enforcement and spawn-only enforcement.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- 21 test files with perfect cohesion and zero coupling represents a healthy, self-sufficient E2E suite — no shared mocks or fixtures are leaking coupling into this layer
- The architecture-policy test (architecture-policy.test.js) is particularly valuable as a living enforcement of the four-tier dependency hierarchy defined in CLAUDE.md; it should be treated as a first-class architectural guardrail
- Coverage spans CLI commands (ci, config, delegation, dev, errors, plan, start, status, sync, usage, work) — verifying that the orchestration tier's contract with end users is fully expressed as executable tests
- architecture-policy.test.js enforces the four-tier import hierarchy at runtime; any new cross-package import path should be validated here first before merging.
- Zero coupling across 21 test files confirms the E2E suite treats the CLI as a true black box, which maximises its value as a refactoring safety net.
- The breadth of CLI command coverage (ci, config, dev, plan, start, status, sync, usage, work) means this zone is the primary regression barrier for the orchestration tier — keeping tests fast and deterministic is essential.
- The e2e suite treats the entire CLI as a black box with zero coupling to any production zone — this means e2e tests cannot be accidentally broken by internal refactors, but it also means they cannot assert on internal state; the architecture-policy test compensates by enforcing structural invariants directly
- architecture-policy.test.js is the only zone in the entire codebase that enforces the four-tier dependency hierarchy as a runtime assertion — it is a single point of failure for import-boundary enforcement; consider whether additional policy checks (e.g. gateway module purity) should live here
- architecture-policy.test.js is the sole runtime enforcer of the four-tier import hierarchy. No secondary enforcement mechanism (lint rule, CI check, gateway purity test) exists. A single deleted or broken test file would silently remove the architectural guardrail for the entire codebase.
- architecture-policy.test.js is a single point of failure for four-tier hierarchy enforcement. If this file is skipped, broken, or omitted from a CI run, cross-tier import violations can merge undetected. The policy should be backed by at least one redundant mechanism (e.g. an eslint import boundary rule or a dedicated CI step that runs before package tests).
- The e2e suite covers CLI commands (ci, config, dev, plan, start, status, sync, usage, work) but has no test for the MCP HTTP transport workflow documented in CLAUDE.md (ndx start → claude mcp add --transport http). The ndx start command is tested, but the MCP session lifecycle and tool invocation over HTTP are not exercised end-to-end.
- cli-delegation.test.js is the closest thing to an architectural enforcement test for the spawn-only pattern at the orchestration tier, but it likely validates delegation behavior rather than import boundaries. The architecture-policy.test.js enforces import boundaries but not the spawn-only rule — there is a gap where orchestration-tier files could add direct library imports without triggering either test.
- Add an e2e test for the complete MCP HTTP workflow: start server, verify /mcp/rex and /mcp/sourcevision respond to tool calls, stop server. This is the recommended transport per CLAUDE.md but has no e2e coverage, meaning regressions in the HTTP MCP layer are invisible to the test suite.
- architecture-policy.test.js enforces cross-tier import boundaries but not the spawn-only rule for orchestration-tier scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js). A companion check that scans orchestration-tier files for require/import statements targeting package internals would close the gap between import-boundary enforcement and spawn-only enforcement.
- [call graph] 435 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
